Good. I can see the namespace is ready with the existing secrets. Now I need to:

1. Create a ConfigMap for Elasticsearch configuration
2. Create a headless Service for node-to-node communication (es-transport or elasticsearch)
3. Create a StatefulSet with 3 replicas
4. Create a Service named `es-http` for HTTPS access on port 9200

Let me design the Elasticsearch cluster configuration. I'll use the TLS secret for HTTPS and the password secret for the elastic user.
For Elasticsearch 8.11.1, I need to enable security with `xpack.security.enabled: true`, configure HTTPS on port 9200 using the `es-http-tls` secret, and set up transport layer security for node-to-node communication. The elastic user password will be injected via the `ELASTIC_PASSWORD` environment variable from the existing secret.

The tricky part is transport TLS on port 9300 — in ES 8.x, security requires it to be enabled, but I can either reuse the HTTP certificate if it has the right SANs for the node hostnames, generate separate transport certificates in an init container, or potentially work around it depending on how the auto-configuration behaves when security is manually configured.

For a test cluster, the simplest approach is to use the same TLS certificate for both HTTP and transport, then set `verification_mode: none` on the transport layer to skip hostname verification and avoid needing specific SANs.

Writing the Elasticsearch configuration manifest... I'm reconsidering the discovery configuration for the StatefulSet. With a headless service, I can either reference the service directly and let Elasticsearch discover all pods automatically, or specify individual pod addresses using the pod DNS naming convention.

Writing Kubernetes manifest... Writing Kubernetes manifests... Configuring the Elasticsearch container... I need to figure out the right discovery seed hosts configuration for Elasticsearch in Kubernetes—using just `es-transport` should work within the same namespace since the DNS will resolve it automatically. I'm also thinking through the storage setup, which should use the default StorageClass in the kind cluster, and I need to verify that the readiness probe is correctly referencing the ELASTIC_PASSWORD environment variable.

The exec probe should inherit the container's environment, so the password variable should be available. I'm refining the readiness probe to use a curl command with basic auth that checks the cluster health status, and I'm also considering how to set the node name dynamically using the pod hostname.

For the Elasticsearch config, environment variable substitution like `${HOSTNAME}` actually does work in elasticsearch.yml for version 8.x, so the pod hostnames would automatically populate the node names. The tricky part is making sure `cluster.initial_master_nodes` matches those actual node names—for a StatefulSet, they'd be `elasticsearch-0`, `elasticsearch-1`, `elasticsearch-2`. I'm also thinking about the `vm.max_map_count` requirement, which needs a privileged init container, though some environments might already have this configured. The headless service needs to properly expose port 9300 for Elasticsearch discovery to work correctly, and I need to ensure the `ELASTIC_PASSWORD` environment variable is set so the elastic user gets the right credentials. input.content:
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: elasticsearch-config
  namespace: elasticsearch
data:
  elasticsearch.yml: |
    cluster.name: "es-cluster"
    node.name: "${HOSTNAME}"
    network.host: "0.0.0.0"
    discovery.seed_hosts: ["es-transport.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local"]
    cluster.initial_master_nodes: ["elasticsearch-0", "elasticsearch-1", "elasticsearch-2"]
    xpack.security.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: none
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-transport
  namespace: elasticsearch
  labels:
    app: elasticsearch
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: elasticsearch
  ports:
    - name: transport
      port: 9300
      targetPort: 9300
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-http
  namespace: elasticsearch
  labels:
    app: elasticsearch
spec:
  selector:
    app: elasticsearch
  ports:
    - name: https
      port: 9200
      targetPort: 9200
---
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: elasticsearch-pdb
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: elasticsearch
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: elasticsearch
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  serviceName: es-transport
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: elasticsearch
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: elasticsearch
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: increase-vm-max-map
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sysctl", "-w", "vm.max_map_count=262144"]
          securityContext:
            privileged: true
        - name: fix-data-perms
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["chown", "-R", "1000:1000", "/usr/share/elasticsearch/data"]
          securityContext:
            runAsUser: 0
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
      containers:
        - name: elasticsearch
          image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 9200
            - name: transport
              containerPort: 9300
          env:
            - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: elastic-password
                  key: password
            - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
              value: "-Xms512m -Xmx512m"
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
            - name: config
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
              subPath: elasticsearch.yml
            - name: certs
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs
              readOnly: true
          readinessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - bash
                - -c
                - |
                  curl -sk -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" \
                    https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health \
                    | grep -qE '"status":"(green|yellow)"'
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
            timeoutSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 12
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "1Gi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:
              memory: "1Gi"
      volumes:
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: elasticsearch-config
        - name: certs
          secret:
            secretName: es-http-tls
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: data
      spec:
        accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 5Gi

Wait - the headless service `es-transport` exists but DNS can't resolve it? Let me check if the issue is that the service has `clusterIP: None` but there are no ready endpoints yet (since the pods aren't ready).

For a headless service, DNS only works when there are ready pods. Since our pods aren't Ready (they fail the readiness probe), the headless service has no endpoints, and DNS returns NXDOMAIN.
 
This is the classic chicken-and-egg problem: the Elasticsearch pods need DNS discovery to form a cluster, but DNS only returns pod IPs when they're Ready, and the pods can't become Ready until the cluster forms. The fix is to enable `publishNotReadyAddresses: true` on the headless service so unready pods still get DNS entries—this is the standard pattern for StatefulSets. I need to patch the es-transport service with this setting.

The StatefulSet is stuck because elasticsearch-0 is not becoming Ready (due to the readiness probe failing since ES isn't fully up yet due to the DNS issue).

With a StatefulSet, by default `podManagementPolicy: OrderedReady` - it creates pods one at a time and waits for each to be ready before creating the next. 

This creates a deadlock: elasticsearch-0 can't become ready without the other nodes to form a cluster. The fix is to switch to `podManagementPolicy: Parallel` so all three pods start simultaneously and can discover each other, or configure the discovery settings to use the individual pod FQDNs from the headless service. The standard approach for Elasticsearch StatefulSets is parallel pod creation, though I need to verify the DNS resolution is actually working first since that was the initial blocker. I'm configuring the seed hosts to use individual pod FQDNs instead of the headless service, which gives more reliable discovery. I'll need to update the ConfigMap with these specific pod addresses, then modify the StatefulSet to add `podManagementPolicy: Parallel` — though that change requires deleting and recreating the StatefulSet since it can't be patched directly.

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: elasticsearch
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  serviceName: es-transport
  replicas: 3
  podManagementPolicy: Parallel
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: elasticsearch
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: elasticsearch
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: increase-vm-max-map
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["sysctl", "-w", "vm.max_map_count=262144"]
          securityContext:
            privileged: true
        - name: fix-data-perms
          image: busybox:1.36
          command: ["chown", "-R", "1000:1000", "/usr/share/elasticsearch/data"]
          securityContext:
            runAsUser: 0
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
      containers:
        - name: elasticsearch
          image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 9200
            - name: transport
              containerPort: 9300
          env:
            - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: elastic-password
                  key: password
            - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
              value: "-Xms512m -Xmx512m"
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
            - name: config
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
              subPath: elasticsearch.yml
            - name: certs
              mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs
              readOnly: true
          readinessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - bash
                - -c
                - |
                  curl -sk -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" \
                    https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health \
                    | grep -qE '"status":"(green|yellow)"'
            initialDelaySeconds: 60
            periodSeconds: 15
            timeoutSeconds: 10
            failureThreshold: 20
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "1Gi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:
              memory: "1Gi"
      volumes:
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: elasticsearch-config
        - name: certs
          secret:
            secretName: es-http-tls
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: data
      spec:
        accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 5Gi
EOF
